lesson.menu_desc: Structure a report with headings, write clear factual sentences and use the passive voice -- 27 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 18 -- Writing a Short Report -- Structure, Headings & the Passive Voice
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Percival JR | Aula 18 -- Writing a Short Report

listenings.text: Hi Percival, this is Karen. Before you write the monthly report, let me brief you on the structure, so it is clear and factual. A short report has three main sections, each with a heading. The first heading is Objective. Here you state the aim in one factual sentence: the objective of this report is to review the safety audit for March. The second heading is Findings. This is the longest section, where the facts and the figures are presented. For example: a survey was carried out on three sites, twelve risks were identified, and the data was checked twice, so the numbers are accurate. Use the passive voice here, because the action matters more than the person: the audit was completed, the results were analysed, the report was written. The third heading is Conclusion. Here the findings are summed up in one or two factual sentences, and a clear recommendation is given. Keep every sentence short and factual, with no opinion. That is the whole structure. When the draft is ready, it will be sent to me for a final check.

Hello Percival, this is Daniel from the audit team, leaving a short voicemail about your report. Thank you for the draft. It was read carefully this morning, and the structure is very good. The three headings are clear: Objective, Findings and Conclusion. A few small points were noticed. In the Findings section, one figure was not accurate, so the number should be checked again. Also, one sentence was written in the active voice, but it would sound more factual in the passive: instead of we collected the data, write the data was collected. Apart from that, the report is well organised and the conclusion is clear. Once the figure is corrected, the report can be approved and it will be filed with the others. Well done, and thank you for keeping it short and factual.

extra_audio.text: First, Percival writes the heading Objective and states the aim of the report in one factual sentence. Then, under the heading Findings, the facts and the figures are presented in the passive voice. After that, each figure is checked twice, so the numbers are accurate. Later, under the heading Conclusion, the findings are summed up and a clear recommendation is given. Finally, the whole report is read once more for accuracy, and then it is sent to Karen for a final check.
